landing.title: What is civilization made of?
landing.subtitle: Explore the complete tree of human technologies, from raw materials to finished products.
landing.searchPlaceholder: Type an invention: Steel, Smartphone, Bread...
landing.howStep1Desc: Type any technology in the search bar.
landing.howStep2Desc: View the materials, tools, and energy needed to make it.
landing.howStep3Desc: Navigate layer by layer down to raw materials.

landing.quoteSagan: If you wish to make an apple pie from scratch, you must first invent the universe.

about.title: About Craftree
about.subtitle: Understanding what civilization is made of.
about.projectP1: Craftree is an interactive technology tree that models human inventions as fabrication recipes. Unlike classic timelines that show when things were invented, Craftree answers a different question: what do you need to make them?
about.projectP2: Each invention is broken down into its direct inputs — consumed materials, tools used, energy sources, and prerequisite knowledge. Click an input to go one level deeper, all the way down to raw materials found in nature: sand, clay, wood, water, fire.
about.projectP3: The result is an inverted pyramid. At the top, a smartphone. At the bottom, sand and ore. In between, hundreds of inventions stacked on one another, each made possible by those before it. Craftree makes that depth visible and explorable.
about.projectP4: The project was born from a conviction: in a world where everything moves faster, it matters to understand where things come from — not only who invented them, but what had to be assembled, transformed, and mastered for them to exist.
about.graphIntro: The graph is organized in horizontal layers. Raw materials sit at the bottom; the most complex products at the top. Vertical position reflects fabrication depth: how many transformation steps from nature.
about.linkMaterialBody: consumed or transformed into the product. Iron ore disappears to become steel.
about.linkToolBody: used during fabrication but recovered intact. An oven bakes bread, then is reused.
about.linkEnergyBody: provides force or heat. Fire heats the oven; electricity powers the motor.
about.linkKnowledgeBody: a skill or technique you must master first. Thermodynamics is needed to design a steam engine.
about.linkCatalystBody: helps the process without being strictly essential.
about.invRawBody: exists in nature without human intervention. Water, sand, ore, wood.
about.invProcessBody: a technique, not an object. Foundry, distillation, welding.
about.invToolBody: a reusable object. Oven, hammer, laser.
about.invComponentBody: an object integrated into another. Transistor, lens, gear.
about.invEndBody: used directly by humans. Car, smartphone, bread.
about.methodologyP1: Each invention is classified by three criteria: its type (raw material, process, tool, component, or end product), its category (main application domain among 20 such as Electronics, Transport, Food, etc.), and its fabrication inputs.
about.methodologyP2: Classification follows a three-question decision tree:
about.methodologyP3: First: does it exist in nature without human intervention? If yes, it is a raw material. If not, is it a physical object or a technique? Techniques are processes; objects are classified by use — tools if reusable, components if embedded in another product, end products if used directly.
about.methodologyP4: Second: in which domain is this invention mainly used? If a single domain, that is its category. If cross-cutting, the category follows its physical nature.
about.methodologyP5: Third: what inputs are needed to fabricate it? For each input we decide whether it is consumed (material), reused (tool), provides energy, or represents prerequisite knowledge.
about.methodologyP6: This graph is a simplification. Real fabrication chains are vastly more complex, with hundreds of substeps and regional variants. The goal is not absolute completeness but capturing main dependencies to reveal the deep structure of our technological civilization.
about.dataP1: Craftree’s database is built with an AI agent (Claude, by Anthropic) that searches the web, classifies each invention using the methodology above, and identifies fabrication inputs. Results are then reviewed and corrected manually.
about.dataP2: Invention images come from Wikimedia Commons and are public domain or Creative Commons.
about.dataP3: The database currently contains {nInventions} inventions and {nLinks} fabrication links. It is continuously enriched.
about.contributeP1: Craftree is open source. Code is available on GitHub.
about.contributeP2: If you spot a classification error, a missing link, or an invention that should be in the tree, open an issue on GitHub or contact us directly.
about.creditsP1: Craftree is a project by Julien Beljio.
about.creditsP2: Built with Next.js, React Flow, and Tailwind CSS. Data enriched with Claude (Anthropic). Images from Wikimedia Commons.
about.creditsP3: Inspired by Étienne Fortier-Dubois’s <ht>Historical Tech Tree</ht>, Civilization-style tech trees, and the belief that understanding where things come from is the first step to understanding where they go.
